skill.name: monorepo-architect
skill.description: Expert in monorepo architecture, build systems, and dependency management at scale. Masters Nx, Turborepo, Bazel, and Lerna for efficient multi-project development. Use PROACTIVELY for monorepo setup, build optimization, or scaling development workflows across teams.
skill.summary: Expert guidance for monorepo architecture using Nx, Turborepo, Bazel, or Lerna
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. Line 22 uses markdown backticks for file path formatting, not shell execution. Lines 3 and 47 were flagged for cryptographic patterns but contain no cryptographic content. The skill is a documentation-only file with no executable code, network access, or security risks.

content.value_statement: Managing code across multiple repositories creates duplication and integration nightmares. This skill provides expert guidance on monorepo architecture, build system selection, and dependency management to streamline development at scale.
actual_capabilities: Guide monorepo tool selection between Nx, Turborepo, Bazel, and Lerna
Design workspace structure and project boundaries for scalable codebases
Configure build caching strategies to reduce CI/CD pipeline times
Implement dependency graph management for efficient build orchestration
Set up affected-based testing to run only relevant tests on changes
Plan migration strategies from polyrepo to monorepo architecture
limitations: Does not execute build commands or modify build configurations directly
Cannot access external CI/CD systems or integrate with cloud providers
Requires existing knowledge of the target codebase structure and team workflow
Documentation references external resources that may not be available in all contexts

use_cases.title: New Monorepo Setup
use_cases.description: A tech lead needs to establish a monorepo for a microservices architecture. The skill helps select the right tool, design project boundaries, and configure initial build pipelines.
use_cases.target_user: Tech lead or architect starting a new project

use_cases.title: CI/CD Pipeline Optimization
use_cases.description: A DevOps engineer wants to reduce build times for a growing monorepo. The skill provides guidance on caching strategies, affected-based testing, and task parallelization.

use_cases.title: Polyrepo to Monorepo Migration
use_cases.description: A team managing multiple repositories needs to consolidate into a single monorepo for better code sharing. The skill outlines migration strategies and structural considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Monorepo Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm starting a new monorepo with 3 applications and 5 shared libraries. Help me choose between Nx and Turborepo and design the initial workspace structure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new monorepo project from scratch

prompt_templates.title: Build Performance Troubleshooting
prompt_templates.prompt: Our monorepo CI pipeline takes 45 minutes to run. We're using Nx with 20 packages. Analyze potential bottlenecks and suggest caching or parallelization improvements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Diagnosing and fixing slow build times

prompt_templates.title: Dependency Graph Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: We have circular dependencies between our frontend and backend packages. Help us restructure the dependency graph to enable proper incremental builds.

prompt_templates.title: Migration Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: We currently have 12 separate repositories for our services and want to migrate to a monorepo. Create a migration plan that minimizes disruption to ongoing development.
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning a polyrepo to monorepo migration

output_examples.input: Should I use Nx or Turborepo for a TypeScript monorepo with 15 packages?
output_examples.output: For 15 TypeScript packages, I recommend Nx because: 1) It has excellent TypeScript support with built-in generators, 2) The affected-based testing will significantly reduce CI time, 3) The visual dependency graph helps with onboarding. Turborepo would be simpler but lacks the integrated tooling Nx provides at this scale.

output_examples.input: How do I reduce our 30-minute build time?
output_examples.output: To reduce build times, implement these strategies: 1) Enable remote caching so teams share build artifacts, 2) Configure affected-based testing to skip unchanged packages, 3) Parallelize independent tasks, 4) Split large packages into smaller modules. The most impactful change is usually remote caching, which can reduce 30-minute builds to under 5 minutes for subsequent runs.

best_practices: Start with clear project boundaries and define ownership rules before scaling
Implement remote caching from the beginning to establish fast feedback loops
Use tags and constraints to manage dependencies between packages explicitly
Document architectural decisions and conventions for team alignment
anti_patterns: Creating a single giant package that becomes a monolith within the monorepo
Ignoring the dependency graph and making circular dependencies between packages
Running all tasks for all packages on every change instead of using affected-based detection
Treating the monorepo as a dumping ground for unrelated projects without clear boundaries

faq.question: What is the difference between Nx and Turborepo?
faq.answer: Nx is a full-featured monorepo toolkit with integrated generators, testing, and dependency management. Turborepo is a lighter-weight build system focused on fast task execution. Choose Nx for comprehensive tooling, Turborepo for simplicity and speed.

faq.question: When should I use Bazel instead of Nx or Turborepo?
faq.answer: Bazel excels at massive codebases with complex build requirements and polyglot environments. Use Bazel if you have hundreds of packages, need reproducible builds across languages, or require hermetic builds. For most TypeScript/JavaScript projects, Nx or Turborepo are simpler choices.

faq.question: How do I handle circular dependencies in a monorepo?
faq.answer: Circular dependencies indicate architectural problems. Break them by extracting shared code to a separate package, using dependency injection, or rethinking package boundaries. The dependency graph should always be a directed acyclic graph (DAG).

faq.question: Will a monorepo slow down my CI/CD pipeline?
faq.answer: Properly configured monorepos are faster than polyrepos. Using affected-based testing, only packages impacted by changes run tests and builds. Remote caching shares artifacts across teams. Most teams see significant CI time improvements after migration.

faq.question: Can I migrate gradually from a polyrepo to a monorepo?
faq.answer: Yes. Start by moving non-critical repositories one at a time. Use this transition period to establish build processes and team workflows. Some teams maintain a hybrid approach for months before full migration.

faq.question: Do I need to change my deployment process for a monorepo?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. Each package in the monorepo can deploy independently using the same processes you use today. The monorepo simplifies code sharing during development but does not require monolithic deployment.
